name: Hungary (Pannonian Basin)
description: The Carpathian basin and Pannonian plain — modern Hungary and surrounding lowlands. Roman Pannonia, then conquered by Magyar tribes in 895; became the Kingdom of Hungary.

periods.note: Roman Pannonia — Augustus conquered the Pannonian basin 35–9 BCE; it became one of Rome's key Danubian provinces (Pannonia Superior and Inferior). Aquincum (modern Budapest) was a major military base. Marcus Aurelius wrote much of his Meditations on campaign here.

ethnic_composition.presence: ruling/administrative; heavily garrisoned
Pannonian Celts — assimilating into Latin culture
Quadi and other Germanic peoples on the Danubian frontier

periods.note: Hunnic then Ostrogothic control — Attila made Pannonia the core of the Hunnic Empire (433–453); after his death the empire fragmented. Ostrogoths then controlled the area before moving to Italy. Gepids and Lombards contested the basin.

ethnic_composition.presence: ruling Hunnic then other steppe groups
Gepids, Lombards competing for control

periods.note: Avar Khaganate — Avars (steppe nomads from Central Asia) conquered the Carpathian basin 568; established a powerful khanate that raided Byzantium and the Franks for two centuries. Charlemagne destroyed the Avar state (791–803), but the plains were then contested between Frankish, Moravian, and Bulgarian spheres.

ethnic_composition.presence: Avar ruling class
subject agricultural population — Slavs flooded the Pannonian basin after the Roman withdrawal

periods.note: Magyar conquest — Seven Magyar tribal confederations under Árpád crossed the Carpathians 895 and conquered the Pannonian basin, displacing Slavic inhabitants or subjugating them. Magyar cavalry raids terrorised Europe (Bavaria, Italy, France, Byzantium) until Otto I's decisive defeat at Lechfeld (955). After Lechfeld the Magyars settled, converted to Christianity, and built a state.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant ruling nomads — transitioning to settled agriculture
significant subject population — absorbed into Hungarian kingdom

periods.note: Christian Kingdom of Hungary — Stephen I received crown from Rome (1000); built a Western-oriented Christian kingdom. Hungary became one of the largest kingdoms in Europe, stretching from Adriatic to Transylvania. Tolerated diverse ethnic and religious minorities ('weak kingdoms speak one language' — Stephen I's advice).

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant
invited settler colonists (Transylvanian Saxons, etc.)
Croats, Slovaks, Serbs — within the Hungarian Crown's territories

periods.note: Mongol Invasion — Batu Khan's army destroyed Hungary's army at Mohi (1241) and devastated the country, killing an estimated half the population. The Mongols withdrew in 1242 (due to Ögedei Khan's death, not Hungarian resistance). Hungary's depopulation was severe enough that King Béla IV resettled Cuman nomads as border guards.

ethnic_composition.presence: conquering force
surviving population — severely reduced

periods.note: Rebuilt Hungary — Béla IV rebuilt the devastated kingdom; stone castle-building programme; Louis I (the Great, 1342–1382) expanded Hungary from the Adriatic to the Black Sea. Matthias Corvinus (1458–1490) was the Renaissance pinnacle. The Turks defeated and killed Louis II at Mohács (1526) — the end of medieval Hungary.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant
urban and mining communities
Croats, Serbs (especially increasing after Ottoman pressure from south)

periods.note: Ottoman Hungary — central Hungary (including Buda) under direct Ottoman rule 1541–1699. Eastern Hungary became Transylvania (semi-independent vassal state). Northwestern Hungary ('Royal Hungary') remained Habsburg. The Battle of Vienna (1683) began the Habsburg reconquest; Treaty of Karlowitz (1699) ended Ottoman Hungary.

ethnic_composition.presence: Ottoman garrison and administration in Buda and central cities
majority of rural population — heavily depopulated by warfare

periods.note: Habsburg Hungary — 'Royal Hungary' (northwest) was under Habsburg rule from 1526; the entire basin reunited under Habsburg control after 1699. Hungarians resisted Habsburg centralisation; Hungarian rebellions (Rákóczi 1703–1711). The Habsburg reconquest brought large-scale German and Serbian colonisation into the depopulated plains.

ethnic_composition.presence: significant — Danubian Swabians resettled the plains; urban burgher class
majority; Magyar nationalism intensifying in 19th century
Slovaks, Croats, Serbs — large minorities within the Kingdom of Hungary

periods.note: Austria-Hungary (Dual Monarchy) — Ausgleich of 1867 gave Hungary equal status with Austria. Hungary governed its half of the empire (including Slovakia, Transylvania, Croatia) with considerable autonomy. Magyar nationalism intensified Magyarisation policies. Habsburg collapse in WWI; Treaty of Trianon (1920) stripped Hungary of two-thirds of its territory.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant in the Hungarian half (c.54% of Kingdom of Hungary's population)
Slovaks, Croats, Serbs, Ruthenians — significant minorities
Transylvanian Saxons and Swabian communities

periods.note: Modern Hungary — Trianon trauma defined 20th-century Hungarian politics. Authoritarian Horthy regime allied with Nazi Germany to recover lost territories; Hungary co-occupied by Germany 1944 and Soviet Red Army. Communist rule 1949–1989; Hungarian Revolution 1956 crushed by Soviet tanks. Transition to democracy 1989.

ethnic_composition.presence: dominant (~90%) — post-Trianon Hungary homogeneous; ethnic Hungarians left as minorities in Romania, Slovakia, Serbia
